title: DPP

trialDesign.design: Multicenter randomized controlled trial (3 arms; troglitazone arm discontinued)
trialDesign.population: 3234 nondiabetic adults with impaired fasting glucose and impaired glucose tolerance
trialDesign.sampleSize: n=3234 (lifestyle vs metformin 850 mg BID vs placebo)
trialDesign.duration: Mean follow-up 2.8 years (stopped early after 65% of planned person-years)
trialDesign.intervention: Intensive lifestyle (≥7% weight loss, ≥150 min/week moderate activity) or metformin 850 mg BID
trialDesign.primaryEndpoint: Incidence of diabetes (1997 ADA criteria, confirmed on repeat testing)
trialDesign.overview: Lifestyle reduced diabetes incidence by 58% and metformin by 31% vs placebo; lifestyle was superior to metformin with NNT 6.9 vs 13.9 over three years.

headings.background: Type 2 diabetes prevention in high-risk adults with elevated fasting and post-load glucose had not been established with pharmacotherapy.
headings.design: Randomized to intensive lifestyle, metformin 850 mg BID, or placebo at 27 US centers.
headings.population: 3234 nondiabetic adults; ~50% minority enrollment; BMI ≥24 (≥22 Asians); IFG and IGT by ADA criteria.
headings.intervention: Lifestyle: 16-lesson curriculum, ≥7% weight loss, ≥150 min/week moderate activity. Metformin: 850 mg BID.
headings.comparator: Placebo BID plus standard lifestyle and dietary recommendations.
headings.followUp: Mean 2.8 years; blinded phase stopped early for efficacy.

outcomes.label: NNT over 3 years
outcomes.interpretation: Lifestyle roughly twice as efficient as metformin

safetyHighlights: GI symptoms most common with metformin; musculoskeletal symptoms highest in lifestyle group
No significant differences in hospitalization or mortality between groups
trialSummary.clinicalBottomLine: Lifestyle modification is the most effective strategy to prevent type 2 diabetes in high-risk adults; metformin is a safe, less potent alternative, especially in younger, heavier patients.

inclusionCriteria: Age ≥25 years; BMI ≥24 (≥22 if Asian); fasting glucose 95–125 mg/dl (≤125 for American Indians)
No prior diabetes diagnosis; not on glucose-altering medications
exclusionCriteria: Medicines known to alter glucose tolerance
Illnesses limiting life expectancy or trial participation
keyTakeaways: Lifestyle ↓58% · metformin ↓31% diabetes incidence vs placebo over 2.8 years.
Effects similar across race/ethnicity; lifestyle superior overall, especially in older and lower-BMI participants.

items.title: DPP — Main Points (High-Yield Overview)
items.content:
Background and Objective: The Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP) investigated whether lifestyle interventions or metformin could prevent or delay type 2 diabetes in high-risk adults.

Study Design: Randomized 3,234 nondiabetic adults with elevated fasting and post-load plasma glucose to placebo, metformin (850 mg twice daily), or intensive lifestyle modification.

Intervention Goals: Lifestyle program targeted ≥7% weight loss and ≥150 minutes/week of moderate physical activity.

Efficacy and Results: Over mean 2.8-year follow-up, lifestyle reduced diabetes incidence by 58% vs placebo; metformin by 31%.

Number Needed to Treat: NNT 6.9 (lifestyle) vs 13.9 (metformin) over three years to prevent one case.

Conclusion: Both interventions were effective and safe; lifestyle modification was significantly more effective than metformin across diverse demographic groups.
keyPoints: Effects similar across race/ethnicity; lifestyle superior overall
items.reference: Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Why did the DPP intentionally design its recruitment to enroll approximately 50 percent minority group members?
items.answer: The researchers aimed to ensure the results could be accurately generalized to the ethnically and culturally diverse United States population, especially since diabetes is particularly frequent in specific minority groups.
keyPoints: The researchers aimed to ensure the results could be accurately generalized to the ethnically and culturally diverse United States population, especially since diabetes is particularly frequent in specific minority groups.
items.reference: DPP Q1: The researchers aimed to ensure the results could be accurately generalized to t... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: How was the primary outcome—the diagnosis of diabetes—clinically confirmed during the trial?
items.answer: Diabetes was diagnosed based on the 1997 American Diabetes Association criteria using an annual oral glucose-tolerance test or a semiannual fasting plasma glucose test; crucially, this required confirmation by a second test, usually within six weeks.
keyPoints: Diabetes was diagnosed based on the 1997 American Diabetes Association criteria using an annual oral glucose-tolerance test or a semiannual fasting plasma glucose test; crucially, this required confirmation by a second test, usually within six weeks.
items.reference: DPP Q2: Diabetes was diagnosed based on the 1997 American Diabetes Association criteria ... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Why was the initial fourth treatment arm, troglitazone, prematurely discontinued in 1998?
items.answer: Troglitazone was removed from the study due to the drug's potential for causing liver toxicity.
keyPoints: Troglitazone was removed from the study due to the drug's potential for causing liver toxicity.
items.reference: DPP Q3: Troglitazone was removed from the study due to the drug's potential for causing ... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: How did the researchers handle participants whose fasting plasma glucose reached or exceeded 140 mg per deciliter during routine monitoring?
items.answer: The assigned study medication was immediately discontinued, and the participant was referred to their personal physician for clinical treatment.
keyPoints: The assigned study medication was immediately discontinued, and the participant was referred to their personal physician for clinical treatment.
items.reference: DPP Q4: The assigned study medication was immediately discontinued, and the participant ... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Why did the independent data monitoring board advise terminating the blinded treatment phase one year early?
items.answer: The board obtained conclusive evidence of the interventions' efficacy based on 65 percent of the planned person-years of observation, rendering further blinded follow-up unnecessary to prove the hypothesis.
keyPoints: The board obtained conclusive evidence of the interventions' efficacy based on 65 percent of the planned person-years of observation, rendering further blinded follow-up unnecessary to prove the hypothesis.
items.reference: DPP Q5: The board obtained conclusive evidence of the interventions' efficacy based on 6... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: How did the clinical effectiveness of metformin vary based on a participant's baseline body metrics?
items.answer: Metformin was significantly less effective in preventing diabetes in participants who had a lower baseline body-mass index (BMI) or a lower fasting plasma glucose concentration.
keyPoints: Metformin was significantly less effective in preventing diabetes in participants who had a lower baseline body-mass index (BMI) or a lower fasting plasma glucose concentration.
items.reference: DPP Q6: Metformin was significantly less effective in preventing diabetes in participant... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Why did the lifestyle-intervention group experience a higher rate of musculoskeletal symptoms compared to the medication groups?
items.answer: This is attributed to the intensive intervention's core goal of engaging participants in physical activity of moderate intensity for at least 150 minutes per week, leading to higher rates of myalgia and arthralgia.
keyPoints: This is attributed to the intensive intervention's core goal of engaging participants in physical activity of moderate intensity for at least 150 minutes per week, leading to higher rates of myalgia and arthralgia.
items.reference: DPP Q7: This is attributed to the intensive intervention's core goal of engaging partici... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: How were the dietary and physical activity modifications delivered to the participants in the lifestyle group?
items.answer: A 16-lesson curriculum was taught on a one-to-one basis by case managers during the first 24 weeks, which was then followed by individualized, flexible monthly individual and group sessions to reinforce behavioral changes.
keyPoints: A 16-lesson curriculum was taught on a one-to-one basis by case managers during the first 24 weeks, which was then followed by individualized, flexible monthly individual and group sessions to reinforce behavioral changes.
items.reference: DPP Q8: A 16-lesson curriculum was taught on a one-to-one basis by case managers during ... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Why did the researchers strictly exclude individuals who were taking medicines known to alter glucose tolerance?
items.answer: To prevent pharmacological confounding variables that could independently affect the primary outcomes of fasting and post-load plasma glucose measurements.
keyPoints: To prevent pharmacological confounding variables that could independently affect the primary outcomes of fasting and post-load plasma glucose measurements.
items.reference: DPP Q9: To prevent pharmacological confounding variables that could independently affect... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: How did the lifestyle intervention impact post-load glucose concentrations compared to the metformin intervention?
items.answer: While both interventions were similarly effective in restoring normal fasting glucose values, the lifestyle intervention proved more effective than metformin at restoring normal post-load glucose values.
keyPoints: While both interventions were similarly effective in restoring normal fasting glucose values, the lifestyle intervention proved more effective than metformin at restoring normal post-load glucose values.
items.reference: DPP Q10: While both interventions were similarly effective in restoring normal fasting gl... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Why is metformin's primary physiological effect observed on fasting plasma glucose rather than post-load glucose?
items.answer: Metformin works primarily by suppressing endogenous glucose production from the liver, which is the main physiological determinant of fasting plasma glucose concentrations.
keyPoints: Metformin works primarily by suppressing endogenous glucose production from the liver, which is the main physiological determinant of fasting plasma glucose concentrations.
items.reference: DPP Q11: Metformin works primarily by suppressing endogenous glucose production from the ... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: How did the trial define the specific physical activity threshold required for the lifestyle intervention group?
items.answer: The defined goal was engaging in moderate-intensity physical activity, such as brisk walking, for a minimum of 150 minutes per week.
keyPoints: The defined goal was engaging in moderate-intensity physical activity, such as brisk walking, for a minimum of 150 minutes per week.
items.reference: DPP Q12: The defined goal was engaging in moderate-intensity physical activity, such as b... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Why was the body-mass index (BMI) eligibility criterion lowered to 22 specifically for Asian participants?
items.answer: The criteria were adjusted (compared to the standard ≥24 for all other groups) to account for differing physiological risk thresholds and body compositions characteristic of the Asian demographic.
keyPoints: The criteria were adjusted (compared to the standard ≥24 for all other groups) to account for differing physiological risk thresholds and body compositions characteristic of the Asian demographic.
items.reference: DPP Q13: The criteria were adjusted (compared to the standard ≥24 for all other groups) t... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: How was participant adherence to the metformin and placebo regimens objectively evaluated throughout the trial?
items.answer: Adherence to the pharmacological treatments was assessed quarterly based on physical pill counts and structured interviews with the subjects.
keyPoints: Adherence to the pharmacological treatments was assessed quarterly based on physical pill counts and structured interviews with the subjects.
items.reference: DPP Q14: Adherence to the pharmacological treatments was assessed quarterly based on phys... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Why were fasting glucose values required to be measured every six months even after a participant was officially diagnosed with diabetes?
items.answer: While oral glucose-tolerance tests were discontinued post-diagnosis, fasting glucose and annual glycosylated hemoglobin were continuously tracked to monitor their glycemic safety and determine if the study medication needed to be halted (if fasting glucose hit ≥140 mg/dl).
keyPoints: While oral glucose-tolerance tests were discontinued post-diagnosis, fasting glucose and annual glycosylated hemoglobin were continuously tracked to monitor their glycemic safety and determine if the study medication needed to be halted (if fasting glucose hit ≥140 mg/dl).
items.reference: DPP Q15: While oral glucose-tolerance tests were discontinued post-diagnosis, fasting glu... — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: What was the average weight loss achieved by the lifestyle-intervention group?
items.explanation: Average weight loss in the lifestyle group was 5.6 kg.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 1: Average weight loss in the lifestyle group was 5.6 kg. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: What percentage of the lifestyle group achieved the primary goal of at least 7% weight loss by the end of the 24-week curriculum?
items.explanation: 50% achieved ≥7% weight loss by 24 weeks.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 2: 50% achieved ≥7% weight loss by 24 weeks. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Which adverse event had the highest incidence rate specifically in the metformin group?
items.explanation: Gastrointestinal symptoms were most common with metformin.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 3: Gastrointestinal symptoms were most common with metformin. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: What was the target maintenance dose of metformin used in the study for the majority of patients?
items.explanation: Metformin was given at 850 mg twice daily.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 4: Metformin was given at 850 mg twice daily. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: The crude incidence of diabetes in the placebo group was how many cases per 100 person-years?
items.explanation: Placebo crude incidence was 11.0 cases per 100 person-years.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 5: Placebo crude incidence was 11.0 cases per 100 person-years. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: The estimated number of persons needed to be treated with metformin for three years to prevent one specific case of diabetes is:
items.explanation: NNT for metformin over three years was 13.9.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 6: NNT for metformin over three years was 13.9. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: What percentage of the randomized study participants were women?
items.explanation: 68% of participants were women.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 7: 68% of participants were women. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: How was self-reported leisure physical activity quantified in the study?
items.explanation: Physical activity was quantified with the Modifiable Activity Questionnaire.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 8: Physical activity was quantified with the Modifiable Activity Questionnaire. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Which treatment group experienced a measurable increase in average fasting plasma glucose values during the first year of the trial?
items.explanation: Average fasting glucose increased in the placebo group during year one.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 9: Average fasting glucose increased in the placebo group during year one. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: By what overall percentage did the intensive lifestyle intervention reduce the incidence of diabetes compared to the placebo?
items.explanation: Lifestyle intervention reduced diabetes incidence by 58% vs placebo.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 10: Lifestyle intervention reduced diabetes incidence by 58% vs placebo. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: What was the mean follow-up duration for the study participants prior to early closure?
items.explanation: Mean follow-up was 2.8 years.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 11: Mean follow-up was 2.8 years. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: What was the strict baseline plasma glucose concentration two hours after a 75-g oral glucose load required for trial eligibility?
items.explanation: Eligibility required 2-hour post-load glucose 140–199 mg/dl.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 12: Eligibility required 2-hour post-load glucose 140–199 mg/dl. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Which of the following best describes the racial and ethnic distribution goal for the trial's participant recruitment?
options: Enroll approximately half the participants from minority groups
Match the exact demographics of the US population
items.explanation: Recruitment targeted ~50% minority representation.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 13: Recruitment targeted ~50% minority representation. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: What was the average recorded decrease in daily energy intake for the lifestyle intervention group after one year?
items.explanation: Lifestyle group decreased energy intake by ~450 kcal/day at one year.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 14: Lifestyle group decreased energy intake by ~450 kcal/day at one year. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Which treatment group showed the greatest preventative advantage in older persons and those with a lower BMI?
items.explanation: Lifestyle was most effective in older participants and those with lower BMI.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 15: Lifestyle was most effective in older participants and those with lower BMI. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: How was the final outcome of the Modifiable Activity Questionnaire mathematically expressed?
options: Average MET-hours per week for the previous year
items.explanation: Activity was expressed as average MET-hours per week for the prior year.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 16: Activity was expressed as average MET-hours per week for the prior year. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: In the lifestyle intervention group, the average fat intake decreased by what percentage of total calories at the one-year mark?
items.explanation: Fat intake decreased by 6.6% of total calories at one year.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 17: Fat intake decreased by 6.6% of total calories at one year. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Which specific glycemic parameter dictated that a patient must permanently discontinue their assigned study medication and seek standard primary care?
items.explanation: Fasting glucose ≥140 mg/dl triggered medication discontinuation.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 18: Fasting glucose ≥140 mg/dl triggered medication discontinuation. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: Based on the subgroup analysis data, what was the risk reduction percentage for the lifestyle intervention in patients aged 60 and over?
items.explanation: Lifestyle reduced diabetes incidence by 71% in participants ≥60 years.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 19: Lifestyle reduced diabetes incidence by 71% in participants ≥60 years. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.question: What percentage of participants in the metformin group took at least 80 percent of their prescribed study medication dose?
items.explanation: 72% of metformin participants took ≥80% of prescribed dose.
items.reference: DPP MCQ 20: 72% of metformin participants took ≥80% of prescribed dose. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: The overall incidence of diabetes was 39 percent lower in the lifestyle-intervention group than it was in the metformin group.
items.explanation: Lifestyle was 39% more effective than metformin in reducing diabetes incidence.
items.reference: DPP T/F 1: Lifestyle was 39% more effective than metformin in reducing diabetes incidence. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: The study concluded that the treatment effects of lifestyle and metformin differed significantly depending on the race and ethnic group of the participant.
items.explanation: Effects were similar across racial and ethnic groups.
items.reference: DPP T/F 2: Effects were similar across racial and ethnic groups. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: Metformin was found to be identically as effective as the intensive lifestyle intervention in restoring normal post-load glucose values.
items.explanation: Lifestyle was more effective than metformin at restoring normal post-load glucose.
items.reference: DPP T/F 3: Lifestyle was more effective than metformin at restoring normal post-load glucose. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: All blood tests during the study were strictly performed without interrupting the assigned treatment, except that the morning dose of placebo or metformin was skipped on the day of the test.
items.explanation: Morning study drug was withheld on test days; otherwise treatment continued.
items.reference: DPP T/F 4: Morning study drug was withheld on test days; otherwise treatment continued. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: At the close of the multi-year study, 99.6 percent of the enrolled participants were still alive.
items.explanation: 99.6% of participants were alive at study close.
items.reference: DPP T/F 5: 99.6% of participants were alive at study close. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: The standard dietary recommendations provided to the medication and placebo groups were based on the Food Guide Pyramid and the equivalent of a National Cholesterol Education Program Step 1 diet.
items.explanation: Placebo/metformin groups received standard dietary counseling per Food Guide Pyramid and NCEP Step 1.
items.reference: DPP T/F 6: Placebo/metformin groups received standard dietary counseling per Food Guide Pyramid and NCEP Step 1. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: The advantage of the lifestyle intervention over metformin was significantly greater in younger persons and those with a higher body-mass index.
items.explanation: Lifestyle advantage was greater in older persons and those with lower BMI; metformin was relatively more effective in heavier patients.
items.reference: DPP T/F 7: Lifestyle advantage was greater in older persons and those with lower BMI; metformin was relatively more effective in heavier patients. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: The statistical design of the study provided 90 percent power to detect a 33 percent reduction from an assumed incidence of 6.5 cases of diabetes per 100 person-years.
items.explanation: Study was powered (90%) to detect a 33% reduction from 6.5 cases/100 person-years.
items.reference: DPP T/F 8: Study was powered (90%) to detect a 33% reduction from 6.5 cases/100 person-years. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: Overall hospitalization and mortality rates were significantly higher in the metformin group compared to the placebo group due to gastrointestinal adverse events.
items.explanation: No significant differences in hospitalization or mortality between groups.
items.reference: DPP T/F 9: No significant differences in hospitalization or mortality between groups. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: To qualify for the study, American Indian clinic participants could be enrolled if their fasting glucose concentration was ≤125 mg per deciliter.
items.explanation: American Indian participants could enroll with fasting glucose ≤125 mg/dl per protocol exception.
items.reference: DPP T/F 10: American Indian participants could enroll with fasting glucose ≤125 mg/dl per protocol exception. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: According to the study's discussion, metformin primarily lowers blood glucose by directly stimulating endogenous insulin secretion from the pancreas.
items.explanation: Metformin primarily suppresses hepatic glucose production, not insulin secretion.
items.reference: DPP T/F 11: Metformin primarily suppresses hepatic glucose production, not insulin secretion. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: The average total weight loss recorded in the placebo group over the course of the study was 0.1 kg.
items.explanation: Placebo group average weight change was 0.1 kg.
items.reference: DPP T/F 12: Placebo group average weight change was 0.1 kg. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: The lifestyle-intervention curriculum was exclusively taught in large group sessions during the first 24 weeks to build community support.
items.explanation: Curriculum was delivered one-to-one during the first 24 weeks, not exclusively in large groups.
items.reference: DPP T/F 13: Curriculum was delivered one-to-one during the first 24 weeks, not exclusively in large groups. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: The proportion of participants who adhered strictly to at least 80 percent of the prescribed study medication dose was slightly higher in the placebo group than in the metformin group.
items.explanation: Placebo adherence (≥80% dose) was slightly higher than metformin (77% vs 72%).
items.reference: DPP T/F 14: Placebo adherence (≥80% dose) was slightly higher than metformin (77% vs 72%). — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.

items.statement: Previous observational studies definitively demonstrated that drugs currently used to treat type 2 diabetes were highly effective for its prevention.
items.explanation: Prior evidence supported lifestyle prevention; drug prevention had not been established before DPP.
items.reference: DPP T/F 15: Prior evidence supported lifestyle prevention; drug prevention had not been established before DPP. — Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med 2002;346:393-403.
